mappings.rationaleAI: Comprehensive survey of Western philosophical schools from Platonism and Aristotelianism (A.1.e, A.1.f) through Stoicism, Epicureanism, and Skepticism (A.1.g-i), Scholasticism (A.1.j), to modern Cartesianism (A.2.a), Empiricism (A.2.b), Rationalism (A.2.c), Kantianism (A.2.e), Idealism (A.2.f), and analytic and continental traditions including Phenomenology and Existentialism (A.2.m-o).

Descartes' foundationalism directly represents Cartesianism (A.2.a), his mind-body dualism connects to different views of mind-body relation including dualism (B.4), his metaphysics of clear and distinct ideas relates to Cartesian metaphysical theory (B.1), and his epistemological foundationalism connects to rationalism as a source of knowledge (C.3).

Theories of thought including Platonism (discerning eternal forms), Aristotelianism (instantiating universals from experience), and computationalism connect to different conceptions of man as knower (B.3), different conceptions of the object of knowledge (C.1), and the status of the universal (C.4).

Aquinas' synthesis of Aristotelianism and Catholic doctrine directly represents Scholasticism (A.1.j) and Thomism as a metaphysical theory (B.1). His natural law ethics connects to theories of conduct (D), and his theism and proofs for God connect to views concerning God's existence (B.2).

Reason as the cognitive capacity for logical inference, with Aristotle's distinction of discursive reason from intuition, connects to Rationalism (A.2.c) and rationalism as a source of knowledge (C.3).
The Enlightenment's philosophical movement covers Empiricism (Locke, Hume) (A.2.b), Rationalism (Descartes) (A.2.c), Kantianism (A.2.e), and Deism and secularism as views concerning God (B.2).

Epistemology examining skepticism, empiricism, rationalism, foundationalism, and coherentism directly maps to theories of thought, knowledge, and faculties of mind (C), including conceptions of the object of knowledge (C.1), validity of knowledge through Kantianism, positivism, and Skepticism (C.2), and sources of knowledge through rationalism and Empiricism (C.3).

Kant's transcendental idealism and critical philosophy directly represent Kantianism (A.2.e), his critical examination of reason connects to Kantianism as a conception of the validity of knowledge (C.2), and his categorical imperative connects to deontological theories and rationalism in ethics (D.2).

Ontology's investigation of being, existence, and categories of reality with frameworks including Platonism and nominalism connects to different types of metaphysical theory (B.1) and the status of the universal (C.4).

Philosophy's core branches (epistemology, ethics, logic, metaphysics) span all major divisions: philosophical schools (A), theories of being (B), theories of knowledge (C), and theories of conduct (D).

The history of philosophy from Presocratic cosmology through Scholasticism, Empiricism, Rationalism, and 20th-century analytic and continental philosophy provides the historical development underlying all major Western philosophical schools (A.1, A.2).

Classical theism's attributes, arguments for God's existence (cosmological, ontological, teleological), and alternative conceptions (pantheism, deism) directly map to different views concerning the existence, attributes, and knowledge of God (B.2).

Consciousness as subjective experience with the hard problem and theories spanning mind-body dualism, identity theory, and phenomenology connects to different conceptions of man as knower (B.3) and different views of mind-body relation (B.4).

Knowledge as true justified belief with foundationalism, coherentism, and skepticism directly maps to theories of thought, knowledge, and faculties of mind (C), conceptions of the object of knowledge (C.1), validity of knowledge (C.2), and sources of knowledge (C.3).

Ethics covering utilitarianism, Kantian deontology, virtue ethics, and metaethical theories directly maps to theories of conduct (D), metaethical theories including intuitionism and naturalism (D.1), and deontological theories (D.2).
Education's aims and theories of knowledge transmission connect to different conceptions of man as knower and maker, including humanism and Pragmatism (B.3).

Aristotle's empiricist methodology and metaphysics of substance directly represent Aristotelianism (A.1.f) and Aristotelianism as a metaphysical theory (B.1). His logical works connecting universals to particulars relate to the status of the universal (C.4).
Cicero's adaptation of Academic Skepticism, Stoicism, and Epicureanism into Latin connects to Stoicism (A.1.g), Epicureanism (A.1.h), and Skepticism (A.1.i).

The Islamic Golden Age's philosophical production bridging Greek antiquity and European Renaissance through Aristotelian synthesis connects to Scholasticism and the role of Islamic philosophers (A.1.j).
The Renaissance's recovery of classical antiquity, including renewed interest in Platonism (A.1.e), and its transition to early modern philosophy connects to the emergence of Cartesianism (A.2.a).
Romanticism's German Idealism and reaction against Enlightenment rationalism connect to Idealism (A.2.f) and Hegelianism (A.2.g).

The scientific method's empirical framework and falsifiability connect to Empiricism (A.2.b) and Positivism and Logical Empiricism (A.2.i), and its philosophical tensions relate to different views of the epistemic status of scientific theories (C.5).

Socrates' dialectical method and ethical focus on justice and virtue connect to the Sophists (A.1.b) as the intellectual context from which Socratic inquiry emerged, and his moral philosophy connects to theories of conduct (D).
Voltaire's introduction of English empiricism to France connects to Empiricism (A.2.b), and his deism connects to different views concerning God including Deism (B.2).

Avicenna's Peripatetic philosophy derived from Aristotle connects to Aristotelianism (A.1.f), and his influence on medieval Islamic theology and European Scholasticism connects to Scholasticism (A.1.j).

Gödel's incompleteness theorems setting limits on formal systems connect to Positivism and Logical Empiricism and metalogical studies (A.2.i), and his results on mathematical provability relate to views of the epistemic status of scientific and mathematical theories (C.5).

Ancient Greek civilization as the origin of Pythagoreanism, Sophism, Eleaticism, Atomism, Platonism, Aristotelianism, Stoicism, Epicureanism, and Skepticism encompasses most ancient philosophical schools (A.1).
Eastern philosophical traditions including Vedanta, Buddhist non-dualism, and Confucian ethics offer alternative conceptions of metaphysical theory (B.1) and of man as knower and doer (B.3).
Political science's empiricist and positivist orientation connects to Positivism and Logical Empiricism (A.2.i) as applied to the study of political phenomena.
Plato's Theory of Forms directly represents Platonism (A.1.e), Platonism as a metaphysical theory (B.1), and Idealism as a conception of the object of knowledge (C.1).
Marx's historical materialism and dialectical method represent dialectical Materialism (A.2.d) and Marxism (A.2.j), and Materialism including dialectical Materialism as a metaphysical theory (B.1).
The history of science tracing empirical inquiry and institutional development provides context for different views of the epistemic status of scientific theories (C.5).
Logic from Aristotelian syllogistics through propositional and modal logic connects to Aristotelianism's foundation of formal logic (A.1.f) and Analytic and Linguistic philosophy (A.2.o).
Psychology studying mind and behavior connects to conceptions of man as knower (B.3) and different views of mind-body relation (B.4).
Time's philosophical and scientific conceptions connect to metaphysical theories addressing the nature of reality, causation, and identity (B.1).
Metaphysics examining being, ontology, causation, and substance with debates on universals and realism directly maps to different types of metaphysical theory (B.1).
Anarchism's anti-capitalist and anti-state philosophy intersects with Marxism (A.2.j) through shared socialist theoretical roots while diverging on the role of the state.
Laozi's concept of the Dao and wu wei offers an alternative to Western Materialist metaphysics (A.2.d, loosely) and represents a non-Western metaphysical theory of reality (B.1).
Ibn Khaldun's empirical social analysis connects to Empiricist tendencies in philosophy (A.2.b) through his systematic observation-based approach to historical and social phenomena.
Machiavelli's empirical analysis of political power and rejection of normative virtue frameworks connects to Realism (A.2.k) and conceptions of man as doer through Pragmatism and rationalism (B.3).
Locke's tabula rasa and experiential origin of ideas directly represent Empiricism (A.2.b) and Empiricism as a source of knowledge (C.3).

Wollstonecraft's grounding of gender equality in Enlightenment rationalism connects to Empiricism (A.2.b) through her rational-empirical approach, and to humanism as a conception of human nature (B.3).
Newton's classical mechanics and mathematical physics connect to Materialist views (A.2.d) and different views of the epistemic status of scientific theories (C.5).
Darwin's theory of evolution connects to Materialism and Naturalism (A.2.d) and to conceptions of man as shaped by natural processes (B.3).

Turing's formalization of computation and the Turing test connect to Analytic and Linguistic philosophy's logical analysis (A.2.o) and to views of mind-body relation through computational theories of mind (B.4).
The history of mathematics from Pythagorean number theory through Greek axiomatic proof connects to Pythagoreanism (A.1.a) as a philosophical school centered on number and reality.
